name: Microsoft Kinect
short_description: A controller-free motion-sensing peripheral for the Xbox 360 that tracks bodies, gestures, and voice using an RGB camera, infrared depth sensor, and microphone array.
long_description: Kinect is a sensor bar that sits beneath a television and uses a structured-light infrared depth camera, an RGB camera, and a microphone array to reconstruct players' skeletons in 3D and recognize their gestures and speech, letting them control games and menus without a handheld controller. Released in 2010 for the Xbox 360, it became the fastest-selling consumer electronics device on record and, beyond gaming, put affordable real-time depth sensing into the hands of researchers and hobbyists. That cheap depth perception seeded a wave of physical-AI work in robotics navigation, 3D scanning, and gesture interfaces, making it a key ancestor of room-scale ambient sensing in everyday spaces.

images.caption: The Microsoft Kinect sensor bar for Xbox 360, showing its RGB camera, infrared depth sensor, and microphone array.
images.alt_text: A horizontal black Microsoft Kinect sensor bar with multiple camera and sensor lenses across its front, mounted on a small motorized base.
